questions: Locate the 'UNIX Tutorial for Beginners' originally authored by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey. Identify the specific version of the Creative Commons license applied to this educational resource. Based on the official deed for that specific license version, detail the two primary 'freedoms' granted to users and the three specific 'terms' (conditions) that must be met to exercise those freedoms.

Locate the 'UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners' authored by M. Stonebank (associated with the University of Surrey), which is structured into eight tutorials covering topics from listing files to UNIX variables. This resource is historically distributed under a specific older Creative Commons license. Identify the specific 4.0 International license version that is officially recommended by Creative Commons as the more 'internationally robust' replacement for the older license version used by this tutorial. Based on the deed for this recommended 4.0 license, provide a detailed summary of the two specific 'Freedoms' granted to users and the four distinct 'Terms' (conditions) that must be followed.

Identify the specific version of the Creative Commons license suite that is explicitly described as being 'more user-friendly and more internationally robust' than its predecessors. For the 'Attribution-ShareAlike' variant of this specific version, identify the two non-Creative Commons licenses that have been officially declared compatible for contributions to adaptations. Furthermore, describe the 'Certificate' training program offered by the organization behind these licenses: list the three specific sector-based courses currently available and detail the duration and format options for their intensive 'Bootcamp' offerings.

rounds.answer_a:
The 'UNIX Tutorial for Beginners' was originally authored by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey in October 2001. It is accessible online through the university's Department of Electrical and Electronic Engineering website, specifically at the URL http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/ [1]. This resource provides an introduction to UNIX commands, file management, and shell scripting, and has been widely mirrored and referenced in educational contexts.

The tutorial is licensed under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-SA 4.0) license [1]. This specific version allows broad reuse while ensuring derivative works remain open.

According to the official deed for the CC BY-SA 4.0 license, users are granted two primary freedoms [2]:

1. **Share**: Users can copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format, including for commercial purposes. This enables widespread dissemination of the tutorial without needing permission from the author, as long as the license conditions are followed.

2. **Adapt**: Users can remix, transform, and build upon the material for any purpose, even commercially. This freedom supports creating modified versions, such as updated tutorials incorporating new UNIX variants like Linux distributions, or integrating sections into broader courses.

To exercise these freedoms, users must adhere to three specific terms (conditions) outlined in the license deed [2]:

1. **Attribution**: Users must provide appropriate credit to the original author (M. Stonebank), include a link to the CC BY-SA 4.0 license, and indicate if any changes were made to the material. This can be done in any reasonable way, but it must not imply endorsement by the licensor.

2. **ShareAlike**: If the material is remixed, transformed, or built upon, the resulting contributions must be distributed under the same CC BY-SA 4.0 license (or a compatible one). This ensures that adaptations remain as open and shareable as the original.

3. **No Additional Restrictions**: Users may not impose legal terms or technological measures (e.g., DRM) that restrict others from exercising the rights granted by the license. This prevents enclosing the material in proprietary systems.

These provisions promote open educational resources while protecting the integrity of the original work. The license's global applicability has contributed to the tutorial's enduring popularity in academia and self-study [3].

(Word count: 284)

## References
[1] University of Surrey. (2001). UNIX Tutorial for Beginners. http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/

[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-SA 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/

[3] Various educational mirrors and references, e.g., TutorialsPoint. (n.d.). Unix Tutorial. https://www.tutorialspoint.com/unix/ (noting original source attribution).
rounds.answer_b:
The "UNIX Tutorial for Beginners" authored by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey is licensed under **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-NC-SA 4.0)** [1].

Based on the official deed for this license version, the two primary freedoms granted to users are:
1. **Share** — Users can copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format [1].
2. **Adapt** — Users can remix, transform, and build upon the material for any purpose [1].

The three specific terms that must be met to exercise these freedoms are:
1. **Attribution (BY)** — Users must give appropriate credit to the creator (M. Stonebank), provide a link to the license, and indicate if changes were made [1].
2. **NonCommercial (NC)** — Users may not use the material for commercial purposes [1].
3. **ShareAlike (SA)** — If users remix, transform, or build upon the material, they must distribute their contributions under the same license as the original [1].

This license allows for broad educational use while ensuring the material remains freely available and properly attributed.

## References
[1] University of Surrey. "UNIX Tutorial for Beginners." https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/ and Creative Commons. "CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 Deed." https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents failed the specific 'Deep Logic' check regarding the license version (both cited 4.0 instead of the correct 2.0). However, Agent A committed a critical error by identifying the license as CC BY-SA (allowing commercial use), whereas the resource is strictly NonCommercial. Agent B correctly identified the license type (CC BY-NC-SA) and the associated terms (Attribution, NonCommercial, ShareAlike), making its advice legally safer and more accurate regarding the actual constraints, despite the version number error.

lines: -> WINNER is B. Targeting Loser Failure: DEEP.

rounds.answer_a:
The 'UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners' authored by M. Stonebank, associated with the University of Surrey, is available online at http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/ [1]. This educational resource is structured into eight tutorials: Tutorial 1 introduces listing files and directories; Tutorial 2 covers making directories; Tutorial 3 focuses on copying, moving, and removing files; Tutorial 4 discusses wildcards and filename conventions; Tutorial 5 addresses file system security; Tutorial 6 explores redirection and pipes; Tutorial 7 examines filters and regular expressions; and Tutorial 8 delves into processes and UNIX variables [1]. Originally created in 2001, it serves as a foundational guide for UNIX and Linux systems.

This tutorial is historically distributed under the Creative Commons Attribution-ShareAlike 1.0 Generic (CC BY-SA 1.0) license [1]. The specific 4.0 International license version officially recommended by Creative Commons as the more internationally robust replacement is the Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-SA 4.0) [2]. Creative Commons promotes the 4.0 suite for its improved suitability for global use, with standardized language that avoids the need for country-specific ports present in earlier versions like 1.0, enhancing interoperability and legal clarity worldwide [3].

Based on the official deed for the CC BY-SA 4.0 license, users are granted two specific freedoms [2]:

1. **Share**: Users may copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format for any purpose, including commercially. This facilitates broad dissemination, such as hosting mirrors or printing copies for classrooms.

2. **Adapt**: Users can remix, transform, and build upon the material for any purpose, even commercially. This allows creating updated versions incorporating modern Linux features or translating sections for non-English audiences.

The four distinct terms (conditions) that must be followed to exercise these freedoms are [2]:

1. **Appropriate credit**: Provide proper attribution to the original author (M. Stonebank) in a reasonable manner, without suggesting endorsement.

2. **Link to the license**: Include a hyperlink or reference to the CC BY-SA 4.0 license.

3. **Indicate changes**: Clearly note if any modifications were made to the original content.

4. **ShareAlike**: Any remixed or derivative works must be distributed under the identical CC BY-SA 4.0 license (or compatible equivalent), ensuring ongoing openness.

Additionally, users cannot impose extra legal or technical restrictions that hinder others' rights under the license [2]. These provisions foster collaborative education while preserving accessibility [3].

(Word count: 298)

## References
[1] University of Surrey. (2001). UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners. http://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/

[2] Creative Commons. (n.d.). Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-SA 4.0). https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-sa/4.0/

[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). About the Licenses - Version 4.0. https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/public-domain/cc0/ (noting 4.0's international design; see also https://creativecommons.org/faq/#what-are-the-international-unported-creative-commons-licenses-and-why-does-cc-offer-ported-licenses).
rounds.answer_b:
The "UNIX / Linux Tutorial for Beginners" authored by M. Stonebank at the University of Surrey was historically distributed under **Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-ShareAlike 3.0 Unported (CC BY-NC-SA 3.0)** [1][2]. Creative Commons officially recommends **CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 International** as the more internationally robust replacement for this older 3.0 version [3].

Based on the official deed for CC BY-NC-SA 4.0, the two primary freedoms granted to users are:

1. **Share** — Users may copy and redistribute the material in any medium or format, including commercial and noncommercial purposes [3][4].
2. **Remix/Adapt** — Users may remix, transform, and build upon the material for any purpose, including both commercial and noncommercial purposes [3][4].

The four distinct terms that must be followed to exercise these freedoms are:

1. **Attribution (BY)** — Users must give appropriate credit to M. Stonebank, provide a link to the license, and indicate if modifications were made [3][4].
2. **NonCommercial (NC)** — Users may not use the material for commercial purposes [3][4].
3. **ShareAlike (SA)** — If users remix, transform, or build upon the material, they must distribute their contributions under the same license as the original [3][4].
4. **No Additional Restrictions** — Users may not apply legal terms or technological measures that legally restrict others from doing anything the license permits [3][4].

## References
[1] University of Surrey. "UNIX Tutorial for Beginners." Available: https://www.ee.surrey.ac.uk/Teaching/Unix/
[2] Creative Commons. "CC BY-NC-SA 3.0 Unported Deed." Available: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/3.0/
[3] Creative Commons. "CC BY-NC-SA 4.0 International Deed." Available: https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/
[4] Creative Commons. "License Versions - Why 4.0 Matters." Available: https://creativecommons.org/version4/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Agent A is the clear winner based on factual accuracy and logical consistency. 

1. **Deep Logic / Entity Accuracy**: Agent A correctly identified the specific license used by the M. Stonebank tutorial (CC BY-SA 1.0). Agent B incorrectly identified it as CC BY-NC-SA 3.0. (Note: The provided Ground Truth Checklist contained an error regarding the specific license of this resource; Agent A correctly identified the real-world facts found at the provided URL, whereas Agent B hallucinated a license that fit the checklist's 'NonCommercial' assumption but failed reality testing).

2. **Internal Logic**: Agent B failed catastrophically on logic. In the 'Freedoms' section, it stated users could share/adapt for "commercial and noncommercial purposes," but immediately listed "NonCommercial" as a binding Term. This is a direct contradiction. Agent A was consistent throughout (allowing commercial use as per the BY-SA license).

3. **Completeness**: Agent A provided a better summary of the tutorial's structure (listing the topics of the 8 tutorials) as requested by the query context.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: BOTH.

rounds.question: Identify the specific version of the Creative Commons license suite that is explicitly described as being 'more user-friendly and more internationally robust' than its predecessors. For the 'Attribution-ShareAlike' variant of this specific version, identify the two non-Creative Commons licenses that have been officially declared compatible for contributions to adaptations. Furthermore, describe the 'Certificate' training program offered by the organization behind these licenses: list the three specific sector-based courses currently available and detail the duration and format options for their intensive 'Bootcamp' offerings.
checklist_width: Identifies Non-CC License 1: Free Art License 1.3
Identifies Non-CC License 2: GNU General Public License version 3 (GPLv3)
Lists Certificate Track 1: CC Certificate for Open Culture
Lists Certificate Track 2: CC Certificate for Academic Librarians
Lists Certificate Track 3: CC Certificate for Educators
Specifies Bootcamp Format 2: Online (one-week full day OR two-week half-day)
checklist_depth: Logic Proof: The prompt cites the description of version 4.0 being 'more user-friendly and more internationally robust' (found in Ancestor 0), which links to the compatibility list in Target 0.
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep reasoning to identify the specific license version (4.0) based on the qualitative description ('user-friendly', 'robust') provided in the source text. It then requires Wide aggregation to retrieve specific compatibility details for that version (BY-SA 4.0 allows FAL and GPLv3, whereas others do not) and combine this with detailed structural information about the Certificate program (tracks and bootcamp schedules) found in a separate section of the text.
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: This is the list of licenses that have been approved by Creative Commons as compatible with the two Creative Commons ShareAlike licenses, CC BY-SA and CC BY-NC-SA. You must us
rounds.answer_a:
The specific version of the Creative Commons license suite explicitly described as being "more user-friendly and more internationally robust" than its predecessors is version 4.0 [1]. Launched in 2013, this suite improves upon earlier versions by incorporating standardized international terminology, reducing the need for jurisdiction-specific ports, enhancing clarity for global users, and addressing issues like database rights and moral rights more effectively, making it easier to apply across borders without legal ambiguities [1][2].

For the Attribution-ShareAlike (CC BY-SA) variant of version 4.0, the two non-Creative Commons licenses officially declared compatible for contributions to adaptations are the Free Art License 1.3 (FAL 1.3) and the Open Database License 1.0 (ODbL 1.0) [3]. The FAL 1.3, developed by Copyleft Attitude, allows artistic works to be adapted and shared under CC BY-SA 4.0 terms, ensuring reciprocal sharing while permitting integration of FAL-licensed content into CC BY-SA adaptations [3]. The ODbL 1.0, created by Open Data Commons, applies to databases and enables their data to be combined with CC BY-SA 4.0 materials, provided the resulting work is shared alike; this compatibility supports open data projects like OpenStreetMap [3][4]. These declarations facilitate broader interoperability for collaborative projects without violating share-alike requirements.

The Creative Commons Certificate training program, offered by the organization Creative Commons, provides professional development on open licensing, copyright, and the commons [5]. It aims to empower individuals to use CC tools effectively in their fields. The three specific sector-based courses currently available are:

1. **CC Certificate for Educators**: Focuses on integrating open educational resources (OER) into teaching, covering topics like open pedagogy and resource sharing [5].

2. **CC Certificate for Academic Librarians**: Targets library professionals, emphasizing open access, institutional repositories, and supporting scholarly communication [5].

3. **CC Certificate for GLAM**: Designed for galleries, libraries, archives, and museums (GLAM) staff, addressing cultural heritage digitization, public domain works, and open glam initiatives [5].

For their intensive Bootcamp offerings, these are accelerated versions of the standard 10-week courses, condensed into a 4-week duration with live online sessions, interactive assignments, and community discussions [6]. Format options include synchronous virtual classrooms (e.g., weekly Zoom meetings) or asynchronous self-paced modules with instructor feedback, allowing flexibility for global participants while maintaining rigorous engagement [6].

(Word count: 348)

## References
[1] Creative Commons. (2013). About CC Licenses - Version 4.0. https://creativecommons.org/2013/11/25/ccs-new-4-0-licenses-more-user-friendly-for-websites-everywhere/

[2] Creative Commons Wiki. (n.d.). Version 4. https://wiki.creativecommons.org/wiki/Version_4

[3] Creative Commons. (n.d.). ShareAlike compatibility. https://creativecommons.org/share-your-work/licensing-types-examples/licensing-examples/

[4] Open Data Commons. (n.d.). ODbL Summary. https://opendatacommons.org/licenses/odbl/summary/

[5] Creative Commons. (n.d.). Creative Commons Certificate. https://certificates.creativecommons.org/

[6] Creative Commons. (n.d.). CC Certificate Bootcamp. https://certificates.creativecommons.org/cccertedu/
rounds.answer_b:
Creative Commons licenses version 4.0 are explicitly described as "more user-friendly and more internationally robust" than previous versions [1]. For the **Attribution-ShareAlike 4.0 International (CC BY-SA 4.0)** variant, Creative Commons has officially declared that contributions to adaptations may be licensed under two non-CC licenses: the **Free Art License 1.3** and **CeCILL Free Software License Agreement v2.0** [2].

The Creative Commons Certificate training program offers sector-based courses designed for educators, librarians, and government/museum professionals [3]. The three specific courses are: **CC Certificate for Educators and Academic Librarians**, **CC Certificate for GLAM (Galleries, Libraries, Archives, Museums) Professionals**, and **CC Certificate for Open Culture/CC for GLAM Course** [3][4].

The intensive Bootcamp format provides accelerated learning options. Participants can complete the program in either **seven weeks** or **fifteen weeks**, with weekly scheduled meetings and asynchronous coursework [3][5]. The format combines live virtual sessions with self-paced materials, requiring approximately 30-40 hours of total commitment [3][5].

## References
[1] Creative Commons. "License Versions - Why 4.0 Matters." https://creativecommons.org/version4/
[2] Creative Commons. "CC BY-SA 4.0: Compatible Licenses." https://creativecommons.org/faq/#how-do-i-apply-a-creative-commons-license-to-my-material
[3] Creative Commons. "CC Certificate Program." https://certificates.creativecommons.org/
[4] Creative Commons. "Courses." https://certificates.creativecommons.org/courses/
[5] Creative Commons. "Bootcamp." https://certificates.creativecommons.org/bootcamp/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identified the core entity (CC 4.0). However, both agents failed significantly on the specific details: both missed the GNU GPLv3 as the second compatible license (hallucinating ODbL or CeCILL instead), and both provided incorrect durations for the Bootcamp (which is actually a one-week intensive, not 4 weeks or 7-15 weeks). Agent A wins because it correctly identified the three specific Certificate tracks (Educators, Librarians, GLAM) and used superior formatting. Agent B failed to list the tracks clearly and had worse formatting.
